turns.text: Nov, if, as I had suggested in my earlier question , this company had done what they should have done legally, put it in a separate account identified as withholding tax , then would that have been property that the trustee may use? It is our belief that the cases that have developed under 10 and under Chapter 12 and primarily, of course, the case of Re=onstru=tion Finance Corporation , on which the have been =arried for ward into the Ban kruptcy Code, and those cases would not appear in Section 543, We believe they were carried forward into Section Sll2( a) . The raa;on for this can be seen from the historical analysis that we have set out in our brief, and which the Court of Appeals followed. There was considerable testimony adduced in the House and also in the Senate wh ere many parties testifyin9 expressed concern that the oriqinal custodial provision, turnover from a custodian under the earlier drafts of the Bankruptcy Code did not provide for turnover by a creditor in self-possession of the debtor 's assets . Following that testimony, the redrafted House bill, H. R. 6 , wa s introduced wh ich containad Section 542(a) in its present form. And we believe that this Reconstruction Finance, but In Re Prudence Bonds , Grand Boulevard Third Avenue Tr3nsit , Colonial Realty, and so on. Judge Kagruder's opinion in Reconstruction Finance the only Court of Appeals opinion treating the particular issue that it did under the old bankruptcy law? KR . RELIN : Well, that particular issue was dealt with in various other cases in other categories , al though I think, as far as I know, Reconstruction Finance was the only case that actually dealt with in v entory, which was the seized assets in that particular case . We that by keying 542(a) to the sale, use , or lease of property under Section 363, Congress intended to en3ble a trustee or reorganizing debtor to acquire po s session of needed property in which the interest of the estate is not inconsequential, so long as th e interests of the party in possession of the property c an b e j udicially determined to be adequately protected. Section 363(e) man da tes an adequate protection hearing the request of the af f ecte d creditor, and in the case of th e p ro p o sed turn o ver of cas h collateral, Section 363 ma ndates such a hea r in g . In case, the trustee h1s the burden of proving the protection offered is adeq u ate . Turnover is required under Section 542(a) of p r operty th1t the trustee =an use , sell , or lease -- it is a disjunctive that is used - - under Section 363 . Although the government has concentrated its analysis o n Subsections (bl and (c) , Subsection (f)(5) of Section 363 permits a trustee to sell property ei t he r in th e ordinary course of business or out of the ordinary course of business free and clear of the inte r est of any othe r entity in the property if " su=h entity could be compelled in a legal or equitable proceeding to accept a money satisfaction of such inte r est ." Clearly , this is the case wi t h respec t to the inte r est of the IRS in all of the property that it seized from Whiting , is the government has no i n terest in the property except to get paid . Therefore, as Whiting could sell the property under Section 363 at (5) , it is entitled to turn over under Section 542( al . Conceding that Section 542 might require t urno ver by ! priv!te creditor in possession of assets , t he gov ernment nonetheless attempts to exclude itself from the !;>Plication of that rule . In essence, the government ' s argument takes the following pattern . There e xisted pre-Bankruptcy Code prior la w governing the effact of pre-bankruptcy tax levies. Nothing in the Co ngressional history specifically indicates that Congress intended to change that prio r la w. !herefore, Congress iid not change the prior law. The govarnment ' s syl l ogism fails, ho wever, of its ovn accord; because it is based on a false major premise . According to the government• s argument, pre-Bankruptcy :oia decisional la v held that the government v as not required to surrender to the trustee for inclusion in the bankruptcy estate property seized prior to bankruptcy to satisfy delinquent taxes. That is the wording in the government's reply brief. But there is no case citei by the government as prior la v under the Bankruptcy Act v hich held that the govern11ent could not be compelled to turn over to a reorganization trustee tangible property seized in a pre-petition tax levy • hich had not been sold at the time of the petition . llith the sole exception of the Pittsburgh Penguin Partners case, all of the prior lav cases concarnad the government's seizure of intangible property and its right to retain that against a liquidation trustee. Pi tt sburgh Penguin Partners, ho wever, which vas a Chapter 11 case, is not e ven prior law .as it vas ie=idei by th e Thlr:I Cir=uit in 1979, one year after the Bankruptcy Code hid been enacted . Moreover, the decisions in American Acceptance Corporation versus and In Re Chantler Bakinq Company , both cited by the• as part of this prior lav, vere published on Karch 30, 1977, and July 18, 1977, respectively, several aonths after the introduction of H. R. 6 on January 4, 1977, v hich alre s:ly contained Section 542Ca) in its present form. Reco9nizin9 as it must that all of the prior cases srose in v here the existence of a bona fide sdverse claim to the property defeated sunmary jurisdiction of the Bankruptcy Court and would have done so for a private credito r in adverse possession as vell as for the qovernaent, the qovernment seeks to e xtend the ratio iecidenii of the csses to broader application by ar9uin9 that the prior cases really held th1 t a tax levy in and of itself effected a transfer of ownership to the government of the property levied upon. But in actuality , the only cases which v ould be prior lsv th1t 1eslt 11 ith that p1rticul1r subject 3re only three cited by the government, United States versus Eiland, s Fourth Circuit 1955 United States versus Sullivan, 1 Third Circuit 1964 case, and this Court ' s decision in !'helps versus United Ststes. Eiland con c erned a prepetition levy on an account receivable v hich belonged to the bankrupt . The 1 cou r t analogized the le v y to a private creditor ' s attachment an:1 gacnishment , and helj that the effect of the federal taxing statutes v as to create a statutory attachment and gacnishment r esulting in a " virt ual t ra ns f er " to the gover nme n t of the rig ht to recei ve p ay ment of the debt . Clearly , the court vould have held t he same v ay fo r ! private att a ching credito r. United States v ersus Sullivan conce r n e d a p repetition levy on t vo insurance companies to obtain the benefits under the policies of a living ta x d e linquent policyholder . Although the court on the authority of Eiland states that a validly invoked seizure is "tantamount to a transfer of o wnership ," and t hose words have been ca r ried on in the subsequen t decisions iealing with this issue under the Ban k ruptcy Code , the court goes on in Sulli van to hold just the It held that by the mere act of levying , t he government was not entitled to have the insurance policies cancelled for their cash values, as the levy did not dress the government with all of the rights of collection process in the cap1city of lienor as distin9uishe d from ovner . Moreover, oo v here in the code is there a provision 9rantin9 to the Commissioner po ver over property interests of delinquents comparable to that 9i ven the trustee in bankruptcy." The last case that is left is Phelps , and we si9nificantly, and the only thin9 I vould like to comment on Phelps to t he Court at this time , is that it is difficult for me to understand the 9overnment's overreliance on Phelps wh en one consijers that the holdin9 most likely vould have been the same , in favor of a secured creditor vho had taken steps to recover the funds at issue from the assi9nee for benefit of creditors had the fonds been the proceeds of the sale of assets, subject to the creditor ' s security interest , rather than proceeds of the sale of assets subject to t he 9overnment ' s unfiled tax lien . Ce r tainly that wou ld have been t he case if the non-tax secured c red itor had seized physical assets and were holding them pursuant1to a lien . MR. RELill : Yes . Exactly. And the rationale appear some what overbroad taken out of context, in All in all , then , it is our opinion that prior decisional lav did not establish a rule that property seized by prepetitioned tax levy was never subject to pos t -bankruptcy turnover . Rather , the cases simply held that the government did not have to turn o v er seized a sse ts in circumstances v here a private creditor e xe r cising equivalent lien rights vould not have had to The true intent of Congress with respect to the t reatment of the Internal Re venue Service as a creditor under the Bankruptcy Code can best be seen by t he provisions of Sec t ion 106(c)(1), vhich in vai ving t he government ' s ;ov ereign immunity makes it clear that any provision of the Code applicable to an entity such as Section 5 4 2(a) , which specifically app l ies to entities , applies to "go v ernmental units . " As the !RS is by far the fede r al governmental u n it most f r eQu2ntly involved in bankruptcy cases, it is clear that Congress intended the IRS to be included in Section 542(a) turnovers , not excluded , as the government here suggests . that a statute ;hould be construed so as to achieve its purpose , that a reform act, such as the Bankruptcy possible si•Plify and •odernize the la w of bankruptcy in conformity with the la w in the field, and thst effa=t shoul1 be 9iven to the svowed intent of the le9islative sponsors to enact le9islation that " encoura9es business reor9anizations by a streamlined ne w commer=ial reor9anization chapter that will protect the investin9 public, protect jobs , and help save troubled businessas ." We believe that the position taken by the 9overn•ent in this case is re9ressive and in conflict v ith the intent of Con9ress in enactin9 the Bankruptcy Code. We respectfully submit that a decision in this =ase in f3vor of the will th wart the independent functionin9 of the bankruptcy system in reor9anization cases by constitutin9 the IRS a censor of prospective Chaptar 11 cases in wh ich it be involved as a creditor, wi th the power to virtually veto by lSSats before petitions can be filed . It will eocoura9e the IRS to seize first and ne9otiate later. Ban kru ptcy Courts will be powerless to preserve the assets of debtors under their jurisidction aqainct forced liquidation e ven if the value of the assets at fiir mlrket exceeds the t1x many times over. Such a result, we believe, could not have been intended by Conqress . lie trust it v ill not be per•itted by this Court . Thank you . The fact that this 11ay th wart the rehabilitation of tha :hbtor is a sorry circumstance in this case , but it proceeds on the basis of the explicit statuto ry word s enacted by Conqress in tha le9islative history . What th e Court of Appeals has done here is to prescribe 1 rule th'l t w3s neither e nacted by Conqress wit h respect to secured creditors but at all events hardly enacted wi th respect to the Internal Revenue Ser v ice 's statutory collection authority .
